PSYCHOLOGY

Emotional Management

💡 Let's face it, investing can be a rollercoaster of emotions. Excitement, fear, greed, and many other feelings constitute the tracks of this amazing roller coaster. While emotions are a natural part of the human behavior, they can be particularly challenging when it comes to investing. They lead to making bad decisions that harm your portfolio's performance. Some people believe that achieving greatness as investors requires them to solely excel in investment skills, but they often overlook the fact that financial markets are primarily a psychological challenge. Let's analyze these challenges and find a way to fight them.

1. Emotions instill stress

Besides increasing your risk of heart disease, depression and other negative effects , stress decreases your cognitive performance. So are you saying that top investors are never stressed? Are they some sort of terminators?

Of course not, but they became masters in managing stress and emotions through the use of different practices, both mental and physical. This ensures that their minds can sustain a long-term high performance which, as you know, is the key to investment success.

If you’re stressed or disturbed by a certain emotion your performance goes down heavily. You won’t be focused and instead prone to making mistakes. So how can you prevent stress and how you can manage your emotions to have better performances?

2. Positive way of thinking

Positive thoughts help redirecting your attention on something that has zero stress. This doesn't mean that you have to ignore life's less pleasant situations. Positive thinking just means that you approach unpleasantness in a more productive way. You think the best is going to happen, not the worst.

Practical example:

You took a big hit on a position.

You now have 2 choices:

  • Accept it and try to improve what didn't work out
  • Refuse it and think that the next investments will also be a failure

What Zero’s do:

  • Think about an event that gave me satisfaction
  • Understand that bad things are just temporary
  • Listen to music that makes my brain release dopamine
  • Convincing myself that I can do everything with persistence and hard work

3. Disconnecting

Social media and technology have forged a dangerous circle where you’re forced to think that you need to be available 24/7. To news, to what your friends do, to market updates etc. This creates the assumption for a high-stress level increase, decreasing productivity. Both the content on your phone and the act of checking it frequently, can trigger a stress response which releases cortisol into the body. Taking a break from the phone and from the market reduces stress and increases future performance.

I slowly started to abandon Instagram and my mind took a big boost. That platform is also just a big smokescreen. People constantly live in a perpetual state of flex. They need to show with which people they’re hanging out, what are they eating, and the last iPhone they bought. All of this just to impress others and tell the world: “Hey, I’m here!”. But ask yourself: are those people really happy or they’re just covering their insecurities?

If you stay on the Gram for most of your day, you might have the perception that other people are living their best life while you're not, creating in your mind a sense of frustration that leads you to less energy. Disconnect from social and your mental health will thank you with a natural energy drink.

4. Working out

Trust me, training has many benefits that I learned exclusively after proving them on my skin:

  • Physical improvement (Your muscles will grow and you'll feel more confident with the world as you’ll be in good shape)
  • Release of dopamine + endorphins (You’ll feel happier)
  • Growth of brain cells
  • Stress and anxiety reduction

My productivity has insanely increased after lifting weights 3x a week. I feel more relaxed, more positive, and the physical improvements have also given me more self-confidence. All of this translates into better analysis and better content shipping as an analyst/creator. For an investor, working out means having a happier and more relaxed mind, therefore making better financial decisions. Try it out and you'll see the results. I promise you.

5. Appreciating what you have

“If only I had a higher-paying job, or a bigger house, or a luxury vacation..”

Many of us live with the anxiety of constantly having more. While it's correct to have goals and aspire to regularly improve our lives, we shouldn’t focus on what we don't have, but on what we have at the moment. Practicing the way of gratitude calms your mind and keeps you focused. Think about:

  • Your old car
  • Your health
  • Your beloved ones
  • The sole fact that you have a roof over your head

These are all aspects of life we tend to take for granted. But life is short..a quick breath and you pass from 20 to 30 in an instant. Then, at some point, one by one, all the things we took for granted slowly start to lose strength and disappear. The old car that efficiently carried you around for years starts to break. Your Superman health of your 20s fades away day by day as if you had held a piece of Kryptonite in your hand. The mother that was annoying you with “Let me know when you come back home” becomes old, leaving you with the desire to spend millions to bring back her youth. If you focus on what you have and learn to be grateful for, the injection of positivity will act as a boost for your future goals.

“Ok, my life ain't perfect, but at least I have my good health. I'll do everything in my power to improve the other aspects!”

If you focus instead on what you don't have, this will forge a death spiral of frustration and negativity, moving you away from your targets. Stop looking at what others have. Be happy for what you have.

6. Reframing perspectives

Reframing perspectives involves changing the way you view a situation or problem by looking at it from a different angle. This can help you see new opportunities or solutions that were previously hidden: “My boss is an idiot” or “The bear market sucks”. These are circumstances that you cannot control, but you can control how you respond to them. List the specific things that are going wrong..most likely you will come up with just a few things. The scope of these stressors will look much more limited than it initially appeared, thus you’ll be able to see things more positively and develop a proper reaction.

Bear market sucks? → More opportunities.

Boss is an idiot? → Gonna work hard and leave my job.

7. Little related story

There was a time when I was working in a kitchen 13/15 hours per day for €1000/1100. Every day was a massacre. Underpaid and forced to work in a place where I was treated like a moron, constantly humiliated. I received plenty of “jokes” with the sole goal of demolishing my self-esteem (don't want to go deeper, they still make me angry thinking about them). During the first month, I poured a lot of tears, constantly asking myself why should I have to suffer so much for such a low wage. But at some point, I realized that those things were unnecessary. Why should I put tears into something that was impossible to change? I used that “energy” to focus on finding solutions to escape that situation. No more attention to the problem, but to the solution.

8. Food for the mind

Take note: I'm not a doctor and my goal isn't to “force” you to take a diet. I'm sharing what I learned and what improved my journey. What we introduce into our body is directly linked to our mind, not only our physical health.

Back in the day, I was eating a lot of trash food and wasn't paying attention to the negative outcomes that it had on my mind. Sugar especially, is a bad drug that damages both your health (increase in fat % / cancer connotations) and mood. I was always tired and depressed, feeling demotivated and not having my brain at 100% of power.

Reducing sugar ~> improving body and mind health ~> better focus and financial decisions.

Believe me or not, once I started to cut off it from my diet, my mental health took a huge pump, just to stay on topic. I feel less tired and I often have a positive attitude during the whole day. What I'm eating during the day (if you're interested in taking the Zero’s diet) :

  • 1/2 eggs per day
  • Organic food (vegetables/fish/meat)
  • 1/2 liters of water per day
  • Matcha Tea + Ginger + Lime
  • Rice and Pasta as carbohydrates
  • 1/2 per week a cheat meal

9. Rejecting negativity

The more you grind on negative thoughts, the more power you give them. Most of our negative thoughts are just that: thoughts, not facts. Our mind is in fact programmed to amplify emotions. If you think negatively, you'll see everything even more negative.

“I’m not like him, he’s better than me”.

Why don’t you say: “He's like me but he worked so much that he’s on a higher level..I will get here too!”? What worked out for me is asking a friend if I wasn't good at “x” situation. Then the truth came out.

10. Facing the market

“Zero, how can I manage the stress of positions? When I sell and see my coin pumping I want to rush back into it.

When I buy and see my coin dumping I start to panic..”

This is one of the most common issues new investors (and not only) face. The market has an outstanding ability: acting as a magnet and frequently forcing you to check it. Additionally, plenty of X accounts leverage your emotions with “Have fun staying poor” “Breaking news” or “You lost the train” statements. This triggers your mind to act and take unnecessary positions going against common logic. Little tips that will make sure you’ll avoid FOMO and bad decisions:

  • Always take a position AFTER well-conducted research from a price action perspective.
  • NEVER rely on news or hype.
    “Damn, they have a partnership with the aliens, this coin will go out of the stratosphere..”

    You start aping in at resistance and you get f***.

  • Once you have sold a position, run away.

Quit every social network or informational source that brings you back to your investment for at least 2 weeks. Go out for a walk and let the dust settle. This will help you to emotionally detach from your investment and save your capital.

What causes 90% of the loss isn't a single buy or sell.

It’s the weakness to rush back into the market.

11. Key takeaways

  • This framework works

No, this ain't a motivational article or something inefficient. These are the things I personally experienced, tested and that I highly recommend to everyone adopting. You'll see over the long term how powerful they can be.

  • Never underestimate your health

All successful people have good habits, both physically and mentally. Taking care of their health increases their productivity which reflects on their business. Do everything in your power to leverage your health.

  • Accept your limits

Understanding our limits (what we know/what we don't) is key to processing a rational approach, thus managing emotions :

You lack in trading ~> don't be upset ~> everyone has their limits ~> be calm and improve your knowledge

Conclusion

I want to point out that emotional management comes also from experience. There are mistakes you need to make and difficulties you need to experience before understanding how to manage the emotions that come from them. The important thing is to welcome them as lessons.

  • My first bear market was brutal, but it gave me strength.
  • My mother's disease was a harsh reality to discover, but it gave me the desire of giving her a better life.

Those were bad situations. But I grew from them.
